How to Download Transcripts for a Whole YouTube Playlist

Updated July 2026 · 4 min read

Quick answer: Paste the playlist link into the TranscriptFlow playlist tool, then pick a format: a ZIP of TXT + SRT files, a single combined TXT, or one combined PDF or Word document. The first 2 videos of any playlist are free — no signup. Full playlists (up to 100 videos each) are a Pro feature at $4.99/month.

Why do this in bulk

Getting one transcript is easy. Getting forty — a whole course, an interview series, a channel's back catalogue — one video at a time is miserable: open each video, expand the description, click "show transcript," copy, paste, repeat. A playlist tool does the whole list in a single pass and hands you one tidy download.

Step by step

  1. Open the playlist on YouTube and copy its URL — it contains list= in the address.
  2. Paste it into the playlist transcript downloader and press Load Playlist.
  3. Every video is listed; the first video's transcript opens in the viewer, and you can switch between videos with the dropdown.
  4. Under Download the whole playlist, choose your format and click. A progress popup shows each video as it's fetched.

Which format should you pick?

  • ZIP (TXT + SRT) — a numbered file per video plus a combined text file. Best when the transcripts feed into other tools: subtitle editors, data pipelines, note apps.
  • Combined TXT — the whole playlist as one plain-text file, built instantly in your browser. Great for pasting into an AI assistant.
  • Combined PDF or Word — the whole playlist as a single readable document, a chapter per video, like the "book" of a course. Ideal for studying, printing, or citing.

Few tools offer that combined single-document option — most only hand you a folder of loose files. If you want one file to read or annotate, that's the one to pick. (See our guide to saving transcripts as PDF or Word for what those documents look like.)

Free vs Pro

You can try any format on the first 2 videos of any playlist for free, with no signup and no card — so you can see the exact output before paying anything. TranscriptFlow Pro ($4.99/month or $29/year) unlocks entire playlists, up to 100 videos each and 1,000 videos per month. Pro skips accounts entirely: you get a license key by email, paste it once, and your browser remembers it.

Popular uses: turning a course playlist into study notes, collecting an interview series for research and citation, archiving a channel's uploads, or feeding a whole series to ChatGPT or Claude for a summary.

Try a playlist — 2 videos free →

Frequently asked questions

Can I download transcripts for an entire YouTube playlist?

Yes — paste the playlist link into the playlist tool and it fetches every video at once, as a ZIP or a single combined PDF/Word document. The first 2 videos are free.

How many playlist videos can I download for free?

The first 2 videos of any playlist, in every format. Pro unlocks full playlists (up to 100 videos each, 1,000/month).

Do I need an account?

No. The free tier needs no signup; Pro uses a license key by email, with no account to create.
